coredata.dc:description: Parkinson’s Disease (PD) stands as a prevalent neurodegenerative disorder. However, current pharmacotherapies for PD face challenges due to inadequate penetration through the blood–brain barrier (BBB), posing limitations on their therapeutic efficacy. Considering the potential of negatively charged carbon dots (CDs) in retaining functional groups from precursor molecules and vertically crossing the BBB, this study focuses on the utilization of fucoidan (FD), a promising pharmaceutical candidate with neuroprotective effects on dopamine-active neurons, for the development of negatively charged CDs through a one-step hydrothermal method, aiming to achieve efficient BBB penetration for PD treatment. The obtained fucoidan-derived carbon dots (FDCDs) exhibit the fundamental characteristics of CDs, such as nanostructure particles with an average diameter of less than 10 nm and significant photoluminescence ability. They also retain the abundant functional groups of SO4 2- from FD, resulting in a negatively charged surface. In vitro cell experiments were conducted to validate the ability of FDCDs to mitigate 1-Methyl-4-phenylpyridinium ion (MPP+)-induced damage in PC12 cells via anti-inflammatory pathway, antioxidant capacity, and anti-apoptotic effect. After confirming the ability of FDCDs to traverse the BBB using 3D small animal imaging, the intravenous administration of FDCDs via tail injection was observed to successfully restore the motor function in 1-Methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP)-induced PD mice. Notably, no apparent biotoxic response was observed, highlighting the promising potential of FDCDs for effective PD therapy.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work. Miaomiao Han and Bingcheng Yi should be considered joint first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Parkinson’s Disease (PD) stands as a prevalent neurodegenerative disorder. However, current pharmacotherapies for PD face challenges due to inadequate penetration through the blood–brain barrier (BBB), posing limitations on their therapeutic efficacy. Considering the potential of negatively charged carbon dots (CDs) in retaining functional groups from precursor molecules and vertically crossing the BBB, this study focuses on the utilization of fucoidan (FD), a promising pharmaceutical candidate with neuroprotective effects on dopamine-active neurons, for the development of negatively charged CDs through a one-step hydrothermal method, aiming to achieve efficient BBB penetration for PD treatment. The obtained fucoidan-derived carbon dots (FDCDs) exhibit the fundamental characteristics of CDs, such as nanostructure particles with an average diameter of less than 10 nm and significant photoluminescence ability. They also retain the abundant functional groups of SO4 2- from FD, resulting in a negatively charged surface. In vitro cell experiments were conducted to validate the ability of FDCDs to mitigate 1-Methyl-4-phenylpyridinium ion (MPP+)-induced damage in PC12 cells via anti-inflammatory pathway, antioxidant capacity, and anti-apoptotic effect. After confirming the ability of FDCDs to traverse the BBB using 3D small animal imaging, the intravenous administration of FDCDs via tail injection was observed to successfully restore the motor function in 1-Methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP)-induced PD mice. Notably, no apparent biotoxic response was observed, highlighting the promising potential of FDCDs for effective PD therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson's disease (PD), characterized by alpha-synuclein aggregation, progressive dopaminergic neuron degeneration, and oxidative stress-induced imbalance of redox homeostasis [1,2], ranks second among neurodegenerative diseases affecting the elderly worldwide [3]. Current treatment approaches for PD primarily focus on symptomatic relief through dopamine replacement therapy [4], with limited efficacy in halting disease progression. The impeded therapeutic effect is primarily attributed to the high semi-permeability of the blood–brain barrier (BBB), which selectively permits the entry of essential nutrients while hindering the penetration of more than 98 % of small-molecule drugs and nearly all large-molecule drugs [5]. Consequently, the predominant mechanisms employed to enhance brain penetration of PD drugs involve nutrient transporter-mediated transport and the design of small lipophilic compounds, but their therapeutic efficacy remains suboptimal [6]. Given that the BBB constitutes a tight structure composed of endothelial cells interacting with pericytes, astrocytes, neurons, and microglia within the neurovascular unit [7], several strategies have been developed to facilitate local drug delivery to the brain by transiently disrupting the tight junctions using osmotic pressure, microbubbles, or ultrasound [8]. However, the temporary breakdown of tight junctions weakens the BBB integrity, potentially leading to uncontrolled drug delivery or the unintended entry of chemicals and toxins into the central nervous system [9]. In this context, urgent efforts are needed to develop novel and effective treatment strategies for PD.

He et al. developed black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNSs) that crossed the BBB to treat PD, which triggered the degradation of α-synuclein. BPNSs also improved mitochondrial dysfunction, reduced levels of reactive oxygen species, and reduced neuronal death and synaptic loss [10]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of high-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm and offer several advantages, including unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, uniform particle size, low synthesis cost, and tunable luminescence [11–13]. These unique features have led to the widespread application of CDs in various biomedical fields, such as biosensors, diagnostics, cancer therapy, drug delivery, and tissue engineering [14–16]. CDs can also retain the functional groups and associated physicochemical properties of their precursor molecules [17], and they have demonstrated potential for a vertical crossing of the BBB when negatively charged [18]. This indicates that negatively charged CDs hold promise as a pharmaceutical candidate for effective PD treatment. Fucoidan (FD), abundantly present in the cell wall matrix of brown seaweed, is characterized by the presence of abundant sulfate radicals (SO4 2−) [17]. FD has been extensively utilized in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and health products owing to its anti-tumor, anti-coagulant, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory effects [19]. Recent studies have also identified FD as a potential agent for brain injury and intervention in neurodegenerative diseases [20]. Gao and Rushendran groups showed that the main mechanisms of FD in the treatment of PD were anti-oxidative stress [21], alleviation of mitochondrial dysfunction, and protection of dopaminergic neurons [22]. It has shown protective effects on dopamine-active neurons in mice treated with 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP)- or lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-treated mice [23]. Therefore, developing negatively charged CDs derived from FD would not only retain the abundant SO4 2− groups that exhibit protective effects on dopamine-active neurons, but also show the potential to efficiently cross the BBB for the treatment of PD. We hypothesize that FD can synthesize fucoidan-derived carbon dots (FDCDs) and SO4 2- can play a role in the treatment of PD.

FDCDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method as previously reported [17]. In brief, after dissolving 0.6 g of FD (Qingdao Bright Moon Seaweed Group, China) in 30 mL of deionized (DI) water, the mixture was swirled for 12 h at room temperature. The obtained 2 % FD solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to treatment at 200 °C for 12 h. To get rid of the insoluble residue, the dark brown solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm after cooling. To obtain FDCDs, the solution was vacuum-dried at 80 °C.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was conducted using the MicJEM-1200EX instrument (Japan) and JEOL 2100PLUS (Japan) to verify the morphology and lattice dimensions of FDCDs. The magnification was 400,000 × and 800,000 ×, respectively. The FDCDs were diluted with DI water, subjected to ultrasonic dispersion for the specified duration, and subsequently examined via TEM. Afterward, 322 particles were randomly chosen for size determination using Image J software (Java 1.6.0_20(64-bit), https://imagej.nih.gov/ij). To characterize the height of FDCDs, atomic force microscope (AFM) measurement was conducted using a Bruker Dimension Icon AFM equipped with SCANASYST-AIR probes. To investigate the properties of CDs, 2 mg of FDCDs were completely dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was subjected to UV–vis spectrophotometry in the range of 200–500 nm using a Shimadzu UV1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The functional groups of FDCDs were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 employing a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 instrument (USA).

For X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of FDCDs, the PHI5000 Versaprobe III ultrahigh vacuum system (operating at 1 × 10-9 bar) equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source and a monochromator was employed. To determine the zeta potential of FD and FDCD samples, a Zetasizer Nano ZSE instrument from the United Kingdom was utilized. Spectrophotometric analysis at 420 nm was performed to measure the SO4 2- content in FDCDs. A standard curve was generated using the reaction between an SO4 2- standard solution from the National Institute of Metrology, China, and a BaCl2 solution obtained from Shanghai Yi En Chemical Technology, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of FDCDs on PD disease in vitro

ce:section.ce:para: PC12 cells (Shanghai Institute of Life Sciences, China) were cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in RPMI 1640 medium (Dalian Meilun Biotechnology, China), supplemented with 10 % horse serum and 5 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). The culture medium was refreshed every two days, and cells were subcultured when reaching approximately 80 % confluence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of the effects of FDCDs on cell viability upon MPP+ stimulation
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of FDCDs on the viability of MPP+-treated PC12 cells, PC12 cells obtained from the Shanghai Institute of Life Sciences, China, were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5000 cells per well and incubated for 12 h. Subsequently, the cells were co-cultured with different concentrations (e.g., 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 mg/mL) of FDCDs for an additional 24 h. Afterward, 800 µM MPP+ (AbMole, Houston, Texas, USA) was introduced for 24 h to induce the creation of a PD cell model. Following PBS washing, each well was supplemented with 100 µL of fresh 1640 RPMI medium containing 10 µL of CCK-8 reagent solution (Dalian Meilun Biotechnology, China). The absorbance of the solutions was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader (SynergyH1/H1M, Bio-Tek, China). Moreover, a Live/Dead Assay was performed. After PBS washing, the MPP+-treated PC12 cells were incubated in 10 mL of PBS solution containing 5 µL of calcein-AM and 5 µL of PI solution (Dalian Meilun Biotechnology, China) for 15–20 min in the dark. Subsequently, fluorescence images were acquired using a Nikon A1 MP fluorescence imaging microscope (Japan). The percentage of living cells and the spreading area of cells were calculated utilizing Image J software.

To examine the possible antagonistic effects of FDCDs against MPP+-induced apoptosis, 1.5 × 105 PC12 cells were seeded per well in 6-well plates, and the cells were grown for 12 h. Subsequently, the cells were −incubated with FDCDs (1 mg/mL) for 4 h. Afterward, the cells were treated with 800 µM MPP+ for 24 h. Following the treatment, the cells were harvested for Western blot analysis to evaluate the expression levels of anti-apoptotic indexes such as Caspase-3, Cleaved Caspase-3, and BCL2-Associated X (Bax), as well as the pro-apoptotic index B-cell lymphoma-2 (Bcl-2) [24]. For Western blot analysis, the cells were homogenized using a lysate (Solaibao, China), and the resulting lysates were centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min at 4 °C. Using the Bicinchoninic Acid Assay (BCA) Protein Assay kit (Solaibao, China), the amount of protein in the supernatant was ascertained. Following 10 % sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE), equal volumes of lysed proteins were separated and then deposited onto polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membranes (Millipore, America). Following blocking, the PVDF membranes were incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies, including rabbit anti-Bcl-2 (diluted 1:5000, Abcam, England), rabbit anti-Bax (diluted 1:5000, Abcam, England), rabbit anti-Caspase-3 (diluted 1:2000, Cell Signaling Technology, USA), rabbit anti-Cleaved caspase-3 (diluted 1:2000, Cell Signaling Technology, USA), and β-actin (diluted 1:10000, Abways, China). Following the incubation of the primary antibody, the membranes were incubated for an additional hour at room temperature using the goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody (diluted 1:10000, Bioeasy, China). Observations and photographs are taken using a gel imaging system (UVP, America). The image J software was used for strip gray analysis. The expression level of the target protein was expressed by the ratio of the β-actin band between the target protein and the internal reference protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of the antagonistic effects of FDCDs against MPP+-induced cell damage
ce:section.ce:para: Using the reactive oxygen species (ROS) Assay Kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China), the amount of intracellular ROS was measured. PC12 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well. Subsequently, the PC12 cells were treated with FDCDs and MPP+ as described in the Live/Dead Assay of Section 2.2.2 . After treatment, the cells were incubated with 10 µmol/L of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) in a serum-free medium for 20 min at 37 °C to measure intracellular ROS levels. Following three washes with serum-free medium, the intracellular ROS level was determined using a microplate reader (SynergyH1/H1M, Bio-Tek, China) with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and an emission wavelength of 525 nm. Additionally, to investigate the impact of the inflammatory microenvironment, the ROS expression in PC12 cells was also assessed under the treatment of 200 μM hydrogen peroxide (H2O2).

Mitochondrial membrane potential in PC12 cells was assessed using the Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Assay Kit with JC-1 (Dalian Meilun Biotechnology, China). PC12 cells were inoculated and treated with FDCDs and MPP+ as described in Section 2.2.2 for WB experimental treatment. Following the treatment, the cells were incubated and washed with JC-1 reagent solution as per the manufacturer's instructions. Subsequently, a laser confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP8, Germany) was used to study the cells and assess the potential of the mitochondrial membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of FDCDs on PD disease in vivo

ce:section.ce:para:
The blood compatibility of FDCDs was assessed using a hemolytic test. Fresh whole blood was obtained from C57BL/6 mice (Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology, China) and centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 5 min. The sediment was washed three times with normal saline (NS) and then resuspended in NS to obtain a 4 % erythrocyte suspension. Subsequently, the erythrocyte suspension was mixed with 1 mL of FDCDs at different concentrations (0.03, 0.3, and 3 mg/mL) and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. Double distilled water and NS were used as a positive control (+) and negative control (−), respectively. After incubation, the treated solutions were centrifuged for five minutes at 5000 rpm, and a Shimadzu UV1800 spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance of the supernatant at 540 nm. The hemolysis rate was calculated as follows.
Hemolysisrate

Where Abs, Abs (−), and Abs (+) represent the absorbance of the FDCDs groups, normal saline (NS) group, and double distilled water group, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the capacity of FDCDs in BBB penetration
ce:section.ce:para: Cy5.5, which contains –NH2 groups (MCE, China), was utilized as a near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) dye to label FDCDs via electrostatic adsorption. In brief, 75 mg of FDCDs was mixed with 25 mL of double distilled water (DW), followed by the addition of 1.9 mg of Cy5.5 dissolved in 5 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The mixture was stirred in the dark for 24 h to synthesize Cy5.5-labeled FDCDs (referred to as FDCDs@Cy5.5). To validate the visualization, the spectral profile of the synthesized FDCDs@Cy5.5 solution was measured using a Shimadzu UV1800 spectrophotometer within the range of 200–800 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence imaging capability of FDCDs@Cy5.5 was assessed using a Small Animal in Vivo 3D Imager (IVIS Spectrum, USA).

To assess the ability of FDCDs to penetrate the BBB, in vivo imaging of FDCDs@Cy5.5 in C57BL/6 mice was conducted using a Small Animal in Vivo 3D Imager (IVIS Spectrum, USA). Two groups of C57BL/6 mice were established for this experiment. One group received an intravenous injection of 200 μL of FDCDs@Cy5.5 at a concentration of 3 mg/mL, while the other group was injected with 200 μL of pristine Cy5.5. Subsequently, fluorescence imaging was performed at regular intervals of 0.5 h to observe the distribution and penetration of FDCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the therapeutic effect of FDCDs on Parkinson's disease
ce:section.ce:para: A PD animal model was built by injecting 30 mg/kg MPTP (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) into the abdomens of C57BL/6 mice, and 3 mg/mL FDCDs dissolved in 0.9 % saline (2 mg/kg FDCDs) was injected through the tail vein to verify the therapeutic effect of FDCDs in PD. Briefly, twenty-four C57BL/6 mice were randomly assigned to the Control group (saline treatment), MPTP group (30 mg/kg MPTP treatment), MPTP + FDCDs group (the treatment of 30 mg/kg MPTP and 2 mg/kg FDCDs) and FDCDs group (2 mg/kg FDCDs treatment). First, the Control group and MPTP group were injected with normal saline through the tail vein and the MPTP + FDCDs and FDCDs groups were injected with FDCDs through the tail vein for three days. The Control group was then given intraperitoneal and tail vein injections of normal saline for five consecutive days. At the same time, the MPTP group was injected with normal saline through the tail vein and MPTP intraperitoneally for five consecutive days. For the MPTP + FDCDs group, FDCDs were injected through the tail vein and MPTP was injected intraperitoneally for 5 consecutive days. For the FDCDs group, FDCDs were injected through the tail vein for 5 days.

After the ninth-day injection of MPTP and FDCDs, the motor coordination and balance of mice were evaluated using a Rotarod Apparatus (Med Associates, USA). Before the test, mice were trained on the apparatus. For the experiment, mice were placed on the rolling rod set at an initial speed of 4 rpm, with an accelerating speed range of 4 ∼ 40 rpm. Three trials were conducted, each with a 20-minute interval between trials. The average latency to fall off the rotarod was recorded as an indicator of the mice's motor balance ability. For the blood routine examination, peripheral blood samples were collected and sent to Qingdao Aidi Animal Medical Institutions for analysis of hematological parameters.

To evaluate the biotoxicity of FDCDs in vivo, vital organs were collected and subjected to hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining following standard protocols. Briefly, paraffin-embedded sections were deparaffinized using xylene and subsequently dehydrated through a series of alcohol gradients. The sections were then stained with hematoxylin (BOSTER Biological Technology, China) and eosin (BOSTER Biological Technology, China). Following staining, the sections were sealed with neutral gum and examined using an optical microscope (Axio Image M2, ZEISS, Germany) to assess any observable changes or abnormalities.

Male C57BL/6 mice (3-month-old, weighing 25–30 g) were obtained from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology, China. The mice were housed in the Laboratory Animal Center of Qingdao University, with a controlled ambient temperature of 23 °C and relative humidity of 55 %. Water and food were provided ad libitum under a 12-hour light/dark cycle.

All data points were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (mean ± SD). GraphPad Prism 8.0 software was used to perform statistical analysis. An analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to compare the differences between the groups, and Tukey's test was then performed. A value of p < 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The functional groups from FD were successfully retained in the synthetic FDCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Through 12 h of hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, FD precursors were pyrolyzed as spherical nanoparticles with uniform dispersibility, as depicted in Fig. 2 A. Prior investigations have verified the hydrothermal method as a bottom-up synthetic route for carbon dots, initially yielding a polymer-like precursor that subsequently undergoes carbonization through an endothermic reaction, leading to the formation of CDs [25,26]. The resulting nanoparticles exhibited an average diameter of 1.83 ± 0.52 nm (Fig. 2 B) and a lattice spacing of 0.221 nm (Fig. 2 C) that corresponded to the (100) planes of carbon [27]. Additionally, the morphology of the nanoparticles displayed a height of 5 nm (Fig. 2 D and 2E), satisfying the nanomaterials standards [28]. Due to the π-π conjugated structure [27], carbon-based nanomaterials typically exhibit excellent UV–Vis absorption. As shown in Fig. 2 F, a high peak was observed at 230 nm (the π-π transition in the aromatic sp2 carbons) in the UV–vis spectrum of the nanoparticles, confirming their CD features. Furthermore, the solution containing the nanoparticles appeared light brown in ambient light but exhibited green-colored luminescence under 365 nm UV light excitation (inset of Fig. 2
F), indicating their significant photoluminescence ability. These findings conclusively demonstrate that the synthesized nanoparticles are CDs (referred to as FDCDs). To verify the presence of retained functional groups and physicochemical properties from FD, the FT-IR spectrum of FDCDs displayed prominent peaks at 3420 cm−1 (OH bond [17]) 2979 cm−1 (CH bonds [29],) 1675 cm−1 (COO− groups), and 869 cm−1 (COS bending vibration), which are associated with the typical biofunctional groups of FD precursor ( Fig. 2 G). Additionally, FDCDs exhibited a negatively charged zeta potential (Fig. 2 H). Zeta potential results showed that both FD and FDCDs were negatively charged, which was attributed to the presence of sulfate groups. Existing studies showed that positively charged particles were more likely to cause apoptosis, while negatively charged particles could inhibit apoptosis, which also proved that FD and FDCDs had anti-apoptosis ability [30,31] Compared with FD, the negative charge of FDCDs was slightly lower. This is because there is some loss of SO4 2- in the process of FD being fired into FDCDs at high temperatures. COO– could also enhance the anti-apoptotic ability of FDCDs due to its ability to reduce oxidative stress [32]. The content of SO4 2- increased proportionally with the concentration of FDCDs (Fig. 2 I). Overall, this study successfully synthesized FDCDs and confirmed the retention of functional groups from FD. XPS spectral data was supplemented and the result revealed a composition of FDCDs with a calculated content of 77.54 % C, 21.06 % O, and 1.40 % S, respectively (Fig. 2J). In contrast to the elemental composition of FD, featuring 36.8 % C, 49.2 % O, and 14.0 % S, the notable augmentation in C content and concurrent reduction in O and S content can be attributed to the carbonization process and the loss of some SO4 2− groups during the high-temperature treatment. Detailed peaks of relevant functional bonds are shown in Table S1 . The OC-O bond (288.86 eV) corresponds to COO − groups. Two peaks at 169.23 and 171.73 eV are attributed to SO4 2− bonds. The OS bond (165.35 ev) corresponds to COS groups ((Fig. 2 K)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FDCDs exhibit the ability to mitigate MPP+-induced damage in PC12 cells
ce:section.ce:para:
MPTP is a potent neurotoxin that can damage neurons in the brain, causing symptoms similar to PD [33]. MPTP is converted into MPP+ when it enters the brain, which is the form in which the potent neurotoxin MPTP exerts toxic effects on dopaminergic neurons in the brain after crossing the BBB and entering the brain [34], causing the apoptosis of dopaminergic neurons in the brain. The mechanism of apoptosis mainly includes the damage of mitochondrial membrane potential and the generation of oxidative stress [35]. Therefore, this study used MPP+ as a modeling drug for in vitro cell experiments to detect the antagonistic effect of FDCDs on MPP+-induced apoptosis to assess the antagonistic effect of FDCDs against MPP+-induced cellular damage. The viability and apoptosis of PC12 cells were evaluated following incubation with MPP+ and FDCDs. As depicted in Fig. 3
A, MPP+ treatment significantly reduced cell viability by 55.80 %, whereas FDCDs effectively mitigated this damage in a dose-dependent manner. The Live/Dead cell assay visually demonstrated the pronounced cell death induced by MPP+, which was significantly inhibited by FDCD treatment (Fig. 3 B). This outcome was corroborated by quantifying the percentage of living cells (Fig. 3 C). Additionally, the remarkable increase in cell spreading area was observed in the MPP+ + FDCDs group compared to Control group (Fig. 3 B, D). Since cell spreading area is closely associated with cell apoptosis [36], the expression of apoptosis-related proteins was investigated (Fig. 3 E). Bax, a well-known pro-apoptotic marker [37], is implicated in mitochondrial membrane permeabilization and impairment of mitochondrial function [38]. This impedes the protective effect of Bcl-2 (an anti-apoptotic marker) on cells, leading to activation of the caspase cascade, including upregulation of pro-apoptotic proteins (e.g., Caspase-3 and Cleaved caspase-3) [39], eventually culminating in cell apoptosis. Recent investigations have demonstrated a close correlation between the Bcl-2 protein and the development of dopaminergic neurons, emphasizing its role in safeguarding these neurons against toxic insults [40]. Furthermore, it has been proposed that neurotoxin-triggered death of dopaminergic neurons is orchestrated through a Bax-dependent apoptotic pathway [41]. In this study, the MPP+ group exhibited significantly increased expression of Caspase-3, Cleaved caspase-3, and Bax compared to the control group (Fig. 3 F-H), indicative of enhanced cell apoptosis induced by MPP+. Conversely, no significant difference was observed in the FDCDs group, suggesting no influence of FDCDs on apoptotic markers. Upon introduction of FDCDs to co-incubated MPP+-treated cells, the expression of apoptosis-related markers significantly decreased in PC12 cells, accompanied by increased Bcl-2 expression and reduced Bax/Bcl-2 ratio (Fig. 3 I-J). Taken together, these results confirm the anti-apoptotic role of FDCDs by counteracting the detrimental effects of MPP+ on cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The diminished cell apoptosis in MPP+-treated PC12 cells strongly correlates with the enhanced mitochondrial respiration induced by FDCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
MPP+ typically impairs cellular mitochondrial respiration by inhibiting complex I, thereby impeding ATP production and triggering ROS generation, ultimately leading to cell damage and apoptosis (Fig. 4
A) [42,43]. High concentrations of H2O2 can contribute to excess oxidative stress and lead to the generation of large amounts of ROS in cells, which thereby leads to cell dysfunction and even cell death [44,45]. H2O2 also causes cell cycle arrest and reduces cell viability, which leads to cell death [46] Oxidative stress also reduces mitochondrial membrane potential and affects mitochondrial function, which further leads to apoptosis [47]. The excessive ROS resulting from oxidative stress is considered one of the contributing factors in PD [48]. To assess intracellular ROS levels, DCFH-DA dye was utilized as an indicator [49]. In healthy PC12 cells, ROS formation remained relatively low. However, after MPP+/H2O2 induction, intracellular ROS levels significantly increased, indicating cellular damage (Fig. 4 B-C). Conversely, incubation with FDCDs demonstrated a dose-dependent reduction in ROS levels in MPP+/H2O2-induced cells. These observations suggest that FDCDs possessed anti-inflammatory properties, which can counteract the detrimental effects of MPP+ by regulating oxidative stress responses. Furthermore, considering the antioxidant function of Bcl-2 [50], it can be speculated that FDCDs may enhance cellular antioxidant capacity by upregulating Bcl-2 expression.

Mitochondria are widely recognized as the primary sites of ROS generation [51]. The mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) serves as an indicator of mitochondrial functional metabolic status, and a decline in MMP signals the onset of mitochondrial damage [52], which has implications for neurological disorders [53]. The MPP+ group exhibited elevated JC-1 monomer expression along with a notable reduction in JC-1 aggregates compared to the Control group (Fig. 4 D), indicating a decrease in mitochondrial membrane potential and successful establishment of the cell damage model. Conversely, no significant differences in JC-1 monomers and aggregates were observed between the FDCDs and the control groups, implying that FDCDs did not adversely affect mitochondrial membrane potential. Furthermore, the addition of FDCDs led to the restoration of mitochondrial membrane potential in MPP+-treated cells to levels similar to those in the control group, suggesting that FDCDs counteracted cell apoptosis by restoring mitochondrial function. This interpretation was further supported by the quantitative analysis presented in Fig. 4 E. All results indicate that the diminished cell apoptosis in MPP+-treated PC12 cells strongly correlated with the enhanced mitochondrial respiration induced by FDCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FDCDs effectively penetrated the BBB to treat the MPTP-induced PD
ce:section.ce:para:
Given the rapid metabolism of nanoparticles in the body [54], mice were administered FDCDs for 3 days before inducing the PD model establishment using MPTP to enhance the protective effect of FDCDs. Afterward, FDCDs were co-injected with MPTP in mice for an additional 5 days to evaluate the ability of FDCDs to mitigate MPTP-induced damage (Fig. 5
A). Firstly, an in vitro hemolysis analysis was performed to assess the biocompatibility of FDCDs. As shown in Fig. 5 B, the hemolysis caused by FDCDs remained below 2 % within the concentration range of 0.03 mg/mL to 3 mg/mL, confirming their favorable hemocompatibility in accordance with ISO 10993–4 guidelines [55]. As the small size of CDs allows them to penetrate the BBB and reach the brain [56], FDCDs were labeled with Cy5.5 to visualize their penetration ability (Fig. 5 C). As expected, FDCDs@Cy5.5 exhibited a noticeable peak at 678 nm, indicating its potential for near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence imaging [28], which was further confirmed by the imaging of near-infrared fluorescence (inset of Fig. 5 C). To verify the capability of FDCDs to cross the BBB, in vivo live imaging was conducted after the injection of Cy5.5 or FDCDs@Cy5.5. No significant fluorescence was observed in the mouse brain after the injection of Cy5.5 for 1.5 h, demonstrating the inability of Cy5.5 to penetrate the BBB (Fig. 5 E). In contrast, fluorescence began to appear in the brain at 0.5 h after the injection of FDCDs@Cy5.5 and persisted for approximately 3 h (Fig. 5 F). This indicated the effective penetration of FDCDs through the BBB into the brain. Besides their nanoscale size, the negative charge of FDCDs also facilitated their BBB penetrability due to the positive charge of the BBB [18]. In the Control group and MPTP + FDCDs group, the weight of mice showed an upward trend. In the MPTP group, there was a slight weight loss on the fifth day compared to the first day. However, compared with the first day, the weight of the FDCDs group increased significantly on the fifth day (p < 0.0001). It was proved that FDCD injection could counteract the weight loss caused by MPTP, and FDCD injection alone had no adverse effect on the weight of mice (Fig. 5 G). Subsequently, motor coordination was evaluated in mice using a rotating rod experiment. The longer the mice stayed on the rod, the better their motor balance [57]. The mice in the Control group, FDCDs group, MPTP group, and MPTP + FDCDs group stayed on the rod for 51.83 s, 31.06 s, 52.17 s, and 42.54 s, respectively (Fig. 5 H). In comparison to the MPTP group, the MPTP + FDCDs group exhibited a 36.96 % increase in rod retention time, indicating improved motor balance in MPTP-treated mice induced by FDCDs. These results demonstrated the potential of FDCDs to penetrate the BBB for PD treatment.

Despite the remarkable advantages of FDCDs, it is necessary to incorporate more biosafety considerations owing to the potential interactions between the FDCDs and the biosystems. Therefore, the potential toxicity towards multiple organs (i.e., heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) was evaluated after injection of FDCDs. Considering the presented reproductive toxicity of CDs [58], testis pathology was also performed in this study. As shown in Fig. 6
A, no morphological and pathological abnormalities were detected in all organs, showing the good histocompatibility of FDCDs. Furthermore, blood routine examination of mice showed no noticeable abnormalities in the lymphocytes (LYM) ratio, monocytes (MONO) ratio, neutrophils (NEU) ratio, red blood cells (RBCs) number, blood platelets (PLT) number, and hemoglobin content, supporting the excellent biosafety in vivo of FDCDs for PD treatment.

In summary, FDCDs were successfully synthesized via the one-step hydrothermal treatment of FD precursors. These FDCDs exhibit essential characteristics of CDs and retain the functional groups (SO4 2-) and associated physicochemical properties (including negative charge) of FDs, enabling their potential to traverse the BBB vertically. In vitro, cell experiments demonstrated the significant protective effects of FDCDs against MPP+-induced damage in PC12 cells, with reduced cell apoptosis strongly linked to enhanced mitochondrial respiration induced by FDCDs. Furthermore, intravenous administration of FDCDs via tail injection was visually observed to facilitate the crossing of the BBB and successfully restored motor function in a PD mouse model, without any observable biotoxic response. These findings underscore the promising potential of FDCDs as a viable therapeutic option for PD treatment.
